item.title: Development Economics for an Age of Upheaval
item.description: The framework that has long guided development economics no longer reflects how modern economies actually create value. As the boundaries that once separated sectors become blurred, prosperity increasingly depends on how economies build the ecosystems that sustain technological progress and nurture new productive capacity.

item.title: The Death of the Degree
item.description: AI is often portrayed as a threat to higher education, with some administrators and professors focused on rooting out its use. But this approach fails to recognize that, in an AI-transformed world, the new dividing lines are technical capabilities and institutional pipelines that can quickly transform research into products.

item.title: Hungary’s Real Economic Test
item.description: Entering the euro with favorable market sentiment, but without an economy that can function well within a monetary union, would not serve Hungary’s long-term interests. It would be a formula for more broken promises—precisely the trend that the new government was elected to end.

item.title: The Iran War Exposes the Global Economy’s Fault Lines
item.description: Buoyed by AI stocks, global financial markets have largely shrugged off the war with Iran. Yet the conflict’s long-term economic costs are only beginning to emerge, as depleted strategic oil reserves, damaged refining capacity, and rising fuel and fertilizer costs increasingly weigh on lower-income economies.

item.title: Governments Should Govern AI, Not Own It
item.description: AI systems are now powerful enough to threaten critical infrastructure, financial systems, and national security. Rather than becoming shareholders in AI companies, as US President Donald Trump has proposed, governments should invest in the independent institutions needed to hold the industry accountable.

item.title: The Tragedy of “Westalgia”
item.description: Some European leaders seem to think that abasing themselves for Donald Trump's pleasure is necessary to save NATO and keep the Western alliance alive. But by so desperately clinging to the good old days of the transatlantic relationship, Europe’s leaders are risking more than just their dignity.

item.title: The West’s Turkey Dilemma
item.description: Few countries better illustrate the difficult trade-off between democratic principles and strategic necessity than Turkey. As the country’s geopolitical and economic importance grows, NATO allies are becoming increasingly willing to overlook President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan’s attacks on democratic institutions.

item.title: How the Tech Lords Hacked the Firm
item.description: A handful of tech oligarchs have figured out how to tap public markets without submitting to shareholder oversight. The recent SpaceX IPO represents the culmination of this process, granting Elon Musk near-total authority and revealing how thoroughly the mechanisms meant to hold corporate leaders accountable have been weakened.

item.title: Asia’s Real Energy Test
item.description: The Iran war has underscored the fragility of global energy markets, exposing which economies can withstand repeated shocks and which cannot. For ASEAN+3 countries, resilience will depend on stronger electricity systems, regional cooperation, and institutions that can manage persistent uncertainty.

item.title: The US Economy Cannot Grow Without Renewables
item.description: Consumption demand, which has long powered America’s formidable economy, rests on the promise of cheap energy. But the Trump administration has conflated that with fossil fuels, confusing historic correlation with causation and, in the process, jeopardizing long-term prosperity.

item.title: The Purpose of Development Cooperation Is to Be Unnecessary
item.description: The original promise of development cooperation was to help countries overcome poverty, build productive economies, and reduce their dependence on external resources. That has not happened, and ongoing reviews of development cooperation, which focus on donor countries' needs and priorities, are unlikely to change this.

item.title: Oil Shocks Are No Longer So Shocking
item.description: Despite this year's US/Israeli-Iran war causing the largest-ever disruption to global oil supplies, the shocks of the 1970s had a greater impact. Because oil has been used as a weapon for so long, markets, policymakers, and strategists have adapted.

item.title: The Mother of All Economic Shocks Is Chinese Mercantilism
item.description: Because analysts tend to put the United States at the center of the global economic story, they have overlooked the single most important force of the past 50 years. But it will be obvious to later generations that Chinese mercantilism has been more globally consequential than any other economic shock or policy choice.

item.title: Letting Russia Burn
item.description: From relentless Ukrainian drone strikes to soaring inflation and fuel shortages, Russia is paying an ever-higher price for Vladimir Putin's war of choice. But the war has become the organizing principle of Putin’s regime, leaving no path to lasting peace without a fundamental political transformation.

item.title: What AI Governance Demands
item.description: AI governance must account for everything from the technology’s material requirements to its effects on communities, businesses, labor markets, the environment, and strategic competition. To this end, a framework of verification-based trust—broader than national regulation and more flexible than a classic treaty—is essential.

item.title: A Japanese Wake-Up Call for America
item.description: Japan’s deepening currency and bond-market woes should be a wake-up call for other countries that appear to be on unsustainable fiscal paths, not least the United States, as well as France, Italy, and the United Kingdom. After all, a crisis in one country often draws investors' attention to others facing similar problems.

item.title: The Israel-Lebanon Recipe for Never-Ending War
item.description: Lebanon’s political circumstances, together with Iran’s strengthened regional position, make Hezbollah’s complete disarmament highly unlikely. By conditioning Israel’s withdrawal from Lebanon on this outcome—but with no provisions about cutting Hezbollah’s Iranian lifeline—the new framework agreement is dead on arrival.

item.title: Why This Energy Shock Is Different
item.description: Unlike recent energy crises, today’s geopolitical shock is destroying supply rather than rerouting it, exposing the limits of the standard policy toolkit. Governments can mitigate its impact on those most exposed, but only if fiscal and monetary policy work in concert.

item.title: China’s Erasure of Tibet Is Accelerating
item.description: In recent years, the international community has stood idly by while China has systematically eradicated Tibetan culture and identity. With the authorities stepping up their assimilation efforts, Asia risks not only losing one of its oldest civilizations, but also facing a China that is bigger, bolder, and more powerful than ever.

item.title: Judging the Enhanced Games
item.description: If fairness in sports required a level playing field that all who wish to compete could afford, that would be a reason for banning expensive performance-enhancing drugs. But it would be a reason for banning expensive high-tech swimming suits and expensive high-tech running shoes, too.
